name: India
description: The world's largest democracy. Uses a parliamentary system where citizens elect Members of Parliament (MPs), and the majority party forms the government.

eligibility.notes: Must be enrolled in the electoral roll and have a Voter ID card (EPIC).

keyDates.title: Election Announcement
keyDates.description: The Election Commission announces dates, triggering the Model Code of Conduct.

keyDates.title: Polling Phases
keyDates.description: Elections happen in multiple phases for security.

keyDates.title: Counting Day
keyDates.description: All votes are counted on a single day.

phases.title: Pre-election Phase
phases.description: Preparing for the massive democratic exercise.

explanations.simple: You need to get a special ID card to prove you are allowed to vote.
explanations.standard: Citizens must enroll in the voter list to get an Electors Photo Identity Card (EPIC) from the Election Commission.
explanations.expert: Registration is continuously updated but stops temporarily after nominations are filed. The ECI uses BLOs (Booth Level Officers) to maintain accurate rolls.

glossary.definition: Electors Photo Identity Card, the official voter ID in India.
Election Commission of India, the autonomous body that conducts elections.

explanations.simple: A set of rules politicians must follow so the election is fair, like not bribing voters.
explanations.standard: Once elections are announced, the Model Code of Conduct comes into effect. It prevents the ruling party from using government resources for campaigning.
explanations.expert: The MCC is not legally binding but the ECI can take strict actions, including countermanding elections, for violations under its Article 324 powers.

glossary.definition: Model Code of Conduct, guidelines issued by the ECI for conduct of political parties and candidates.

simple.question: Who is in charge of running elections in India?
simple.explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is the special group in charge of making sure elections are fair.

standard.question: Which body conducts national elections in India?
standard.explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an independent constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes.

expert.question: Under which Article of the Constitution does the ECI derive its power to supervise elections?
expert.explanation: Article 324 vests the superintendence, direction, and control of elections in the Election Commission.

explanations.simple: Parties release a promise book telling people what they will do if they win, and hold huge gatherings.
explanations.standard: Political parties release their election manifestos outlining their promises. Leaders travel across the country holding massive public rallies.
explanations.expert: Campaign expenditure is strictly capped for individual candidates, though there is no limit on what the political party itself can spend.

simple.question: When do politicians have to stop campaigning before voting day?
simple.explanation: Campaigning must stop 2 days (48 hours) before polling to give people a quiet time to decide.

standard.question: When does campaigning usually stop before polling day?
standard.explanation: Campaigning must stop 48 hours before the end of polling to give voters a 'silent period' to reflect.

expert.question: Is there a legal cap on how much a political party can spend during a campaign?
options: Yes, strict caps exist for both parties and candidates
No, there are caps for candidates but not for parties
Caps only exist for state elections
expert.explanation: While individual candidates have a strict expenditure limit, there is currently no limit on what the political party itself can spend.

explanations.simple: Instead of paper, people press a button on a machine next to their favorite party's symbol.
explanations.standard: Voters use Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs). They press a button against the candidate's name and symbol. A slip is printed to confirm the vote.
explanations.expert: EVMs are standalone, non-networked machines. The VVPAT (Voter Verified Paper Audit Trail) system allows voters to verify their vote, adding a layer of auditability.

glossary.definition: Electronic Voting Machine, used universally in Indian general and state elections.
Voter Verified Paper Audit Trail, a printer attached to the EVM that shows the vote cast.

simple.question: How do most people cast their vote in India today?
options: Pressing a button on an EVM
simple.explanation: Most voting in India is done by pressing a button on an Electronic Voting Machine (EVM).

standard.question: What does VVPAT do?
options: Prints a paper slip confirming the vote
standard.explanation: VVPAT prints a paper slip that the voter can see through a glass for 7 seconds to verify their vote was recorded correctly.

expert.question: Are Indian EVMs connected to the internet?
options: Yes, through a secure government network
expert.explanation: EVMs are standalone, non-networked machines designed to prevent remote hacking or tampering.

explanations.simple: The person with the most votes in an area wins. The party that wins the most areas gets to rule.
explanations.standard: India uses the First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) system. The candidate with the most votes in a constituency wins. A party needs a majority in the Lok Sabha (272 seats) to form the government.
explanations.expert: In FPTP, a candidate doesn't need an absolute majority (>50%), just more votes than anyone else. This can sometimes lead to a mismatch between vote share and seat share.

glossary.definition: First-Past-The-Post, an electoral system where the candidate with the most votes wins.
The lower house of India's bicameral Parliament.

simple.question: What does a candidate need to win in their area?
options: Just more votes than the 2nd place person
simple.explanation: In India's system, you just need to get more votes than anyone else running against you in that area to win.

standard.question: How many seats are needed for a simple majority in the Lok Sabha?
standard.explanation: The Lok Sabha has 543 elected seats, so a party or coalition needs at least 272 seats for a majority.

expert.question: A key characteristic of the FPTP system is:
options: It perfectly matches vote share to seat share
A candidate can win without an absolute majority (>50%)
expert.explanation: In FPTP, the candidate with the most votes wins, even if that plurality is less than 50% of the total vote cast.

faqs.question: What is NOTA?
faqs.answer: NOTA stands for 'None of the Above'. It's an option on the EVM that allows a voter to reject all candidates. However, it doesn't invalidate the election even if it gets the most votes.

faqs.question: How are illiterate voters able to vote?
faqs.answer: Every political party and candidate has a unique, standardized pictorial symbol (like a lotus, hand, or elephant) on the EVM, making it easy to identify them without reading.
